Paper Title: Anomaly Detection and Classification in Power System State Estimators
Problem Statement: Bad data (BD), sudden load changes (SLC), and false data injection attacks (FDIA) evade traditional χ²-tests in power grids
Methodology/Algorithm: χ²-test + anomaly detection index + supervised ML (random forest, SVM) for classification
Advantages: Detects anomalies eluding traditional methods; topology-agnostic; no retraining after network changes
Evaluation Metrics: Accuracy on IEEE 14-bus; Precision/Recall for each anomaly type (BD, SLC, FDIA); F1-score
Limitations: Requires extensive labeled datasets; limited to 14-bus test system; high computational cost

Paper Title: Intelligent IoT Platform for Multiple PV Plant Monitoring
Problem Statement: Real-time anomaly detection in distributed PV plants; next-day generation prediction accuracy varies by site
Methodology/Algorithm: Five AI models (LSTM, XGBoost, SVR, RF, GB) for prediction; adaptive anomaly detection on IoT sensor streams
Advantages: Integrates prediction + anomaly detection; handles multiple plants simultaneously; IoT-edge processing
Evaluation Metrics: RMSE, MAE on next-day forecast; precision/recall for faults; inference time
Limitations: Requires IoT infrastructure; model retraining needed; limited to solar-only systems

Paper Title: Data Driven Machine Learning Model for Condition Monitoring and Anomaly Detection in Power Grids
Problem Statement: Increasing renewable penetration requires automated fault/anomaly identification; manual methods infeasible at scale
Methodology/Algorithm: Synthetic data generation + supervised ML (DT, RF, SVM, NB, GB); low linear complexity and memory footprint
Advantages: Model-free; efficient memory usage; synthetic training enables scalability
Evaluation Metrics: Accuracy, precision, recall, AUC-ROC on synthetic data
Limitations: Over-reliance on synthetic data quality; limited real-world validation; domain generalization unclear

Paper Title: Fault Detection and Diagnosis of Photovoltaic Systems Using CNN-Bi-GRU
Problem Statement: PV system faults (module degradation, inverter failures) cause undetected output drops; early detection improves reliability
Methodology/Algorithm: CNN for spatial feature extraction + Bi-GRU (bidirectional) for temporal patterns; three-step heuristic optimization
Advantages: Captures bidirectional temporal patterns; 25% reliability gain; robust to weather variability
Evaluation Metrics: Precision=0.94, Recall=0.91, F1=0.92 on real PV data
Limitations: Hyperparameter sensitivity; real-world data scarcity; computational overhead

Paper Title: Leak Event Diagnosis for Power Plants: Generative Anomaly Detection Using Prototypical Networks
Problem Statement: Constrained data collection (few labeled normal samples) in hazardous facilities; traditional anomaly detection fails
Methodology/Algorithm: Generative Anomaly Detection using Prototypical Networks (GAD-PN); few-shot learning paradigm
Advantages: Requires only limited normal samples; no need for extensive labeled anomaly data; one-class learning
Evaluation Metrics: Few-shot detection rate; anomaly identification accuracy; false positive rate
Limitations: Few-shot paradigm untested at scale; power plant-specific; limited generalization to other domains

Paper Title: A Hybrid Framework for Forecasting Power Generation of Multiple Renewable Energy Sources
Year & Publisher: 2024, Renewable and Sustainable Energy Reviews Elsevier
Problem Statement: Existing forecasts ignore correlations among solar PV, solar thermal, and wind in hybrid systems; ignores joint variability
Methodology/Algorithm: A-LSTM (attention layer) + CNN (correlation mining) + linear layer; captures nonlinear temporal + spatial correlations
Advantages: Captures multivariate dependencies; outperforms single-source models; sensitivity analysis of component combinations
Evaluation Metrics: RMSE, MAE on hybrid generation; improvement vs. LSTM-only and CNN-only baselines
Limitations: Complex tuning; requires aligned multi-source data; regional validation limited

Paper Title: Short-term Wind Power Prediction Using Hybrid Neural Networks
Problem Statement: Wind power variability causes grid instability; single-model forecasts have 15-20% error; hybrid methods needed
Methodology/Algorithm: Deep learning ensemble: LSTM + GRU + temporal attention; multi-step ahead prediction
Advantages: Handles multi-step horizon; ensemble reduces overfitting; attention weights interpretable
Evaluation Metrics: RMSE <7% on test set; MAE; directional accuracy
Limitations: Computationally expensive; hyperparameter tuning intensive; limited to hourly data

Paper Title: Anomaly Detection on Smart Grids With Optimized Autoencoders
Problem Statement: Grid anomalies (faults, cyberattacks, sensor failures) require early detection; autoencoder reconstruction errors alone insufficient
Methodology/Algorithm: Optimized autoencoders (dropout, batch normalization) + threshold learning; semi-supervised training on normal periods
Advantages: Robust to concept drift; low false positive rate; interpretable reconstruction error
Evaluation Metrics: Precision, Recall, F1-score; ROC-AUC; anomaly detection delay
Limitations: Sensitivity to threshold choice; reconstruction error non-uniform across features; retraining overhead

Paper Title: Advanced Anomaly Detection in Energy Control Systems Using Machine Learning
Problem Statement: Industrial control systems (ICS) in energy plants vulnerable to cyber-physical attacks; real-time detection needed
Methodology/Algorithm: Gradient boosting + feature engineering (domain-specific); low latency inference architecture
Advantages: Domain-aware features reduce false alarms; real-time processing (<500ms)
Evaluation Metrics: True positive rate, false positive rate at scale; latency benchmarks; detection speed
Limitations: Feature engineering labor-intensive; domain-specific tuning required; generalization limited

Paper Title: Semi-Supervised Anomaly Detection Applied to Solar Power Generation Data
Problem Statement: Limited labeled solar anomaly data; supervised learning infeasible; unsupervised has high false positives
Methodology/Algorithm: Semi-supervised framework: Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) + isolation forest; learns from mixed labeled/unlabeled data
Advantages: Reduces labeling burden by 70%; combines generative + discriminative; practical for real plants
Evaluation Metrics: F1-score on held-out labeled data; precision/recall trade-off; cost of labeling reduction
Limitations: Requires assumption of Gaussian normal data; sensitivity to anomaly prevalence; GMM parameter tuning

Paper Title: Vision Transformer-Based Anomaly Detection in Smart Grids
Problem Statement: Traditional time series models miss spatial patterns in distributed grids; transformers offer global attention mechanisms
Methodology/Algorithm: Vision Transformer (ViT) applied to grid state snapshots; self-attention captures multi-scale dependencies
Advantages: Self-attention interpretable; global receptive field; handles non-Euclidean grid topology
Evaluation Metrics: Anomaly detection accuracy; attention weight visualization; computational cost
Limitations: Transformer overhead (~5x LSTM latency); quadratic complexity with grid size; requires grid graph structure

Paper Title: Large-Model-Based Smart Agent for Time Series Anomaly Detection in Power Systems
Problem Statement: Dual challenges: data scarcity + concept drift; existing methods struggle with zero-day anomalies
Methodology/Algorithm: Large language model (LLM) as smart agent; SLEP framework + BRIDOR prompt design; in-context learning
Advantages: First application of LLM for power time series; handles novel anomalies via prompting; adaptive without retraining
Evaluation Metrics: Anomaly detection F1; comparison vs. classical/DL baselines; human-in-loop evaluation
Limitations: LLM hallucination risk; high inference cost; requires domain-specific prompt engineering

Paper Title: Anomaly Detection in Smart Grid Time-Series Data Using Semi-Supervised Learning
Problem Statement: Normal operation patterns vary; unsupervised + supervised hybrids offer flexibility; real-world labeled data expensive
Methodology/Algorithm: One-class SVM + local outlier factor (LOF) ensemble; semi-supervised threshold optimization
Advantages: Learns from unlabeled normal data; low computational cost; interpretable decisions
Evaluation Metrics: True/false positive rates; detection latency (<100ms); memory footprint
Limitations: One-class SVM kernel tuning sensitive; LOF neighborhood parameter critical; scalability to millions of points

Paper Title: Smart Anomaly Detection in Solar Power Plants with Autoencoder-LSTM
Problem Statement: Sequential anomalies (multi-day faults) missed by point methods; solar plants need proactive maintenance
Methodology/Algorithm: Autoencoder + LSTM: reconstruction error on sequential patterns; temporal attention for fault localization
Advantages: Detects 5-20 day fault progressions; maintenance scheduling enabled; 40% cost savings reported
Evaluation Metrics: Anomaly sequence F1; maintenance cost reduction; false positive rate (<5%)
Limitations: Tuning reconstruction threshold per plant; LSTM requires sufficient normal history; location accuracy limited

Paper Title: Combining IoT and Time Series Model for Minute-Level Outlier Detection in Wind Speed Forecasting
Problem Statement: Wind speed anomalies (turbulence, sensor faults) degrade minute-level forecasting; real-time detection needed
Methodology/Algorithm: ARIMA + residual analysis + IoT anemometer integration (1-minute data)
Advantages: Real-time; minute-level granularity; novel IoT fusion; no pre-training needed
Evaluation Metrics: Anomaly count (87 detected); RMSE improvement with outlier correction; temporal accuracy
Limitations: IoT infrastructure required; single-site validation; ARIMA stationarity assumption

Paper Title: Forecasting Energy Production for Hybrid Solar-Wind Systems Using Transformers
Problem Statement: Solar + wind variables correlated and non-stationary; transformer attention captures temporal + inter-variable dependencies
Methodology/Algorithm: Transformer encoder with multi-head attention; positional encoding for solar/wind sequences; joint forecasting
Advantages: Outperforms LSTM by 18%; handles variable-length context; attention weights identify key drivers
Evaluation Metrics: RMSE <8% on hybrid systems; MAE; skill score vs. persistence
Limitations: Transformer complexity; data requirement (~3 years); hyperparameter count (40+)

Paper Title: Anomaly Detection and Classification in Solar Panels Using Deep Learning
Problem Statement: Panel-level faults (hotspots, bypass failures) invisible to string-level monitoring; classification of fault types needed
Methodology/Algorithm: ResNet-based CNN for thermal imaging + LSTM on electrical signals; multi-modal fusion
Advantages: Multi-modal (thermal + electrical); identifies fault type (hotspot, bypass, soiling); explainable CNN saliency
Evaluation Metrics: Precision=0.96, Recall=0.93 for hotspots; fault type F1=0.89; thermal sensitivity
Limitations: Requires thermal camera infrastructure; labeling thermal imagery labor-intensive; occlusion sensitivity

Paper Title: Predicting and Optimizing Solar, Wind, and Diesel Energy for Hybrid Ships
Problem Statement: Marine hybrid systems have coupled environmental effects; wind/wave/current correlations not modeled; efficiency loss
Methodology/Algorithm: Multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) + neural network forecasting; coupled differential equations for environment
Advantages: Captures physical coupling; energy efficiency 22% higher; adaptive to changing seas
Evaluation Metrics: Operating cost reduction; fuel consumption baseline; power balance violations (<<1%)
Limitations: RL training slow (~weeks); marine-specific; limited to case study vessel

Paper Title: A Review of Solar and Wind Energy Forecasting: From Single- to Multi-site Methods
Problem Statement: Survey of 200+ forecasting methods; state-of-the-art deep learning solutions classified; gap analysis between research/practice
Methodology/Algorithm: Survey structure: taxonomy of methods (statistical, ML, DL); literature classification by site count, horizon, data frequency
Advantages: Comprehensive; identifies 10 open research questions; practical recommendations
Evaluation Metrics: Citation analysis; method categorization; gap identification
Limitations: Review-only (no novel method); snapshot of 2025 landscape; continuous evolution of field

Paper Title: The Hybrid Renewable Energy Forecasting and Trading in Real Markets
Problem Statement: Probabilistic forecasting for wind-solar aggregates enables trading revenue; quantile forecasting often overlooks hybrid correlation
Methodology/Algorithm: Probabilistic forecasting (quantile regression forests + neural networks); day-ahead market simulation
Advantages: First trading-focused hybrid study; achieves substantial revenue; addresses real market constraints
Evaluation Metrics: Trading revenue (€/MWh); probabilistic forecast skill (CRPS); liquidity
Limitations: Market-specific (NordPool case); regulatory changes invalidate model; portfolio bias
